one_liner: A 58-year-old woman with longstanding hypertension and type 2 diabetes presents for routine follow-up with worsening fatigue, nocturia, and bilateral leg swelling.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient presents for her annual physical. She has been feeling generally well but reports mild fatigue over the past several months, occasional nocturia (2-3 times per night), and mild bilateral ankle swelling by the end of the day. She denies gross hematuria, foamy urine, flank pain, or changes in urinary frequency. Her diabetes has been suboptimally controlled with HbA1c values around 8.5% over the past 2 years. Her blood pressure at home readings have been consistently elevated around 150/90. She is adherent to most medications but admits to missing doses occasionally.
stage2.pmh: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (12 years), hypertension (15 years), hyperlipidemia, obesity.
stage2.medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily, glipizide 10 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily.
stage2.family_history: Father with ESRD on dialysis. Mother with type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Brother with CKD stage 4.
stage2.social_history: Administrative assistant. Non-smoker. No alcohol or illicit drugs. Sedentary lifestyle. Diet high in processed foods.
stage2.physical_exam: Obese woman in no acute distress. Fundoscopic exam: cotton-wool spots and dot-blot hemorrhages bilaterally. Heart: regular rate and rhythm, S4 gallop, no murmurs. Lungs: clear bilaterally. Abdomen: obese, soft, non-tender, no masses. Extremities: **1+ bilateral pitting edema at the ankles**; **Peripheral pulses diminished bilaterally**; **Decreased monofilament sensation in bilateral feet**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Elevated and rising; indicates progressive kidney disease
eGFR 30-44 defines CKD stage 3b; significant loss of kidney function
Moderately increased albuminuria (A2 category); indicates glomerular damage likely from diabetic nephropathy
Suboptimal glycemic control contributing to progressive diabetic nephropathy
Mildly elevated; impaired renal potassium excretion in CKD
Mild metabolic acidosis from reduced acid excretion in CKD
Elevated phosphorus from reduced renal excretion; risk for CKD-mineral bone disease
Mild hypocalcemia from decreased 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D production by failing kidneys
Mild normocytic anemia of CKD from reduced erythropoietin production
Secondary hyperparathyroidism from hypocalcemia and hyperphosphatemia in CKD
Suboptimally controlled hyperlipidemia; cardiovascular risk increased in CKD
Vitamin D deficiency, common in CKD; contributes to secondary hyperparathyroidism

imaging_results.result: Bilateral kidneys mildly reduced in size (right 9.8 cm, left 9.5 cm) with increased cortical echogenicity. No hydronephrosis, masses, or stones.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Small echogenic kidneys suggest chronic parenchymal disease; increased echogenicity correlates with fibrosis

low_yield_tests: Renal artery Doppler (no clinical suspicion for RAS)

stage5.final_clue: An eGFR of 38 mL/min/1.73m² with albuminuria (UACR 320 mg/g), bilateral small echogenic kidneys, and complications of CKD (anemia, hyperphosphatemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, metabolic.

debrief.classic_presentation: A middle-aged patient with longstanding hypertension and diabetes is found to have progressively declining eGFR, albuminuria, and early CKD complications (anemia, mineral bone disease, metabolic acidosis) on routine labs. Diabetic retinopathy supports diabetic nephropathy as the etiology.
debrief.pathophysiology: CKD is defined as kidney damage (albuminuria) or decreased kidney function (eGFR <60) for ≥3 months. Diabetic nephropathy is the most common cause of CKD/ESRD in the United States. Chronic hyperglycemia causes glomerular hyperfiltration, mesangial expansion, and progressive glomerulosclerosis. Hypertension accelerates nephron loss through arteriolar damage. As functioning nephrons decline, complications emerge: reduced EPO production (anemia), decreased phosphate excretion and vitamin D activation (CKD-MBD with secondary hyperparathyroidism), reduced acid excretion (metabolic acidosis), and impaired potassium handling (hyperkalemia).
debrief.treatment_overview: Blood pressure control to <130/80 using an ARB (losartan or valsartan, since patient cannot tolerate ACE inhibitors) for nephroprotection. Optimize glycemic control (target HbA1c <7%). Consider SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin) for cardiorenal protection. Treat metabolic acidosis with sodium bicarbonate supplementation if bicarb <22. Manage CKD-MBD with phosphate binders, vitamin D supplementation, and dietary phosphorus restriction. Correct anemia with iron supplementation first, then consider ESA if Hgb remains <10. Low-sodium, low-potassium, low-phosphorus diet. Nephrology referral for co-management. Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, contrast). May need to adjust or discontinue metformin if eGFR falls further.
high_yield_pearls: CKD staging: Stage 1 (eGFR ≥90 with kidney damage), Stage 2 (60-89), Stage 3a (45-59), Stage 3b (30-44), Stage 4 (15-29), Stage 5 (<15 or dialysis).
The presence of diabetic retinopathy in a patient with diabetes and CKD strongly supports diabetic nephropathy as the etiology, often obviating the need for biopsy.
SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) have robust evidence for slowing CKD progression and reducing cardiovascular events, independent of glycemic control.
ACE inhibitors or ARBs are first-line antihypertensives in CKD with proteinuria because they reduce intraglomerular pressure and slow disease progression.
Referral to nephrology is recommended when eGFR <30, rapidly declining eGFR, persistent albuminuria, or when CKD complications are difficult to manage.
sources: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Chronic Kidney Disease — https://kdigo.org/guidelines/
ADA Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes — Microvascular Complications and Foot Care (2024) — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search
UpToDate: Overview of the management of chronic kidney disease in adults (2024) — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search

reasonable_ddx: Chronic kidney disease from diabetic nephropathy
after_stage2_more_likely: Diabetic nephropathy — 12-year history of poorly controlled diabetes with retinopathy, strong family history of CKD
Hypertensive nephrosclerosis — longstanding hypertension contributes; likely a coexisting factor
after_stage2_less_likely: Obstructive uropathy — no symptoms of urinary obstruction, no palpable bladder
Polycystic kidney disease — no family history of PKD, no flank masses on exam
after_labs_top3: Diabetic nephropathy — progressive albuminuria, declining eGFR, retinopathy, small echogenic kidneys in longstanding DM
Hypertensive nephrosclerosis — significant contributor given uncontrolled HTN; likely overlapping with diabetic nephropathy
Chronic glomerulonephritis — less likely given classic diabetic picture with retinopathy and no active sediment
